questions.stem: A life insurance policy was issued on June 1, 2024. The insured dies by suicide on March 15, 2025. The policy contains a standard 2-year suicide exclusion. What will the insurer pay?

options.label: A
options.text: The full face amount, because suicide is always covered under life insurance

options.label: B
options.text: Nothing, because suicide voids the policy entirely and no benefit is ever payable

options.label: C
options.text: A return of premiums paid, because the death occurred within the suicide exclusion period

options.label: D
options.text: Half the face amount as a compromise settlement

questions.correct_explanation: The standard suicide clause provides that if the insured dies by suicide within two years of the policy's issue date, the insurer's liability is limited to a return of premiums paid. The policy was issued June 1, 2024, and the death occurred March 15, 2025, which is within the 2-year exclusion period. After the exclusion period expires, suicide is covered like any other cause of death.

wrong_explanations.A: Suicide is not always covered. During the exclusion period (typically the first two years), the insurer's obligation is limited to returning premiums paid. Coverage for suicide applies only after the exclusion period has elapsed.
wrong_explanations.B: The suicide clause does not void the policy entirely or deny all payment. It limits the insurer's liability to a return of premiums paid during the exclusion period. The beneficiary still receives the premium refund.
wrong_explanations.D: There is no half-benefit compromise provision in the suicide clause. The insurer returns the premiums paid, not a fraction of the face amount.

questions.stem: A whole life policyholder failed to pay premiums and the policy lapsed 8 months ago. The policyowner now wants to reinstate the policy. Under the standard reinstatement provision, which of the following is the insurer MOST LIKELY to require?

options.label: A
options.text: A new application with full underwriting at current age, as if the policy had never existed

options.label: B
options.text: Evidence of insurability satisfactory to the insurer and payment of all past-due premiums with interest, provided the policy has not been surrendered and any automatic nonforfeiture insurance remains in force

options.label: C
options.text: Only the payment of one current month's premium, with no evidence of insurability

options.label: D
options.text: Reinstatement is not permitted once a life insurance policy has lapsed for more than 30 days

questions.correct_explanation: For a standard life policy subject to Texas nonforfeiture rules, reinstatement is allowed within three years after default if the policy has not been surrendered, any other insurance purchased with the policy value remains in force, the insurer receives evidence of insurability satisfactory to the insurer, and arrears of premiums are paid with interest. This restores the original contract rather than issuing a brand-new policy.

wrong_explanations.A: Reinstatement restores the original policy rather than requiring a completely new policy to be issued at current age. The insurer may require evidence of current insurability, but the contract being revived is the original policy.
wrong_explanations.C: Payment of one current premium alone is insufficient. The insurer generally requires all overdue premiums with interest and satisfactory evidence of insurability before reinstating a lapsed life policy.
wrong_explanations.D: Texas life policies subject to the standard reinstatement provision allow reinstatement well beyond 30 days. The standard period is three years after default, assuming the other statutory conditions are met.

status_history.notes: Aligned reinstatement rule to Texas life policy provision: three-year window; removed unsupported policy-loan requirement and 3-to-5-year wording.

questions.stem: A participating whole life insurance policyowner receives an annual policy dividend. Which of the following is a standard dividend option?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the dividend to purchase one-year term insurance (paid-up additions option)

options.label: B
options.text: Apply the dividend to increase the policy's guaranteed interest rate

options.label: C
options.text: Use the dividend to reduce the policy's death benefit

options.label: D
options.text: Use the dividend to purchase additional paid-up whole life insurance

questions.correct_explanation: One standard dividend option allows the policyowner to use dividends to purchase small amounts of additional paid-up whole life insurance. This increases the total death benefit and cash value without additional underwriting. Other standard options include receiving dividends in cash, applying them to reduce premiums, leaving them on deposit with interest, or purchasing one-year term additions.

wrong_explanations.A: Using dividends to purchase one-year term insurance is known as the fifth dividend option or the one-year term option, not the paid-up additions option. The description in this answer mislabels the option name. The paid-up additions option purchases permanent whole life coverage, not one-year term.
wrong_explanations.B: Dividends cannot be used to change the guaranteed interest rate stated in the policy. The guaranteed rate is a contractual provision set at issue and is not adjustable by dividend election.
wrong_explanations.C: Dividends are intended to benefit the policyowner, not reduce the death benefit. No standard dividend option results in a lower death benefit.

questions.stem: An insurance agent tells a prospective client that a policy covers a specific condition, even though the policy language clearly excludes it and the insurer never authorized the agent to make that promise. If the client relies on the agent's statement and purchases the policy, the agent's unauthorized representation may bind the insurer under which type of authority?

options.label: A
options.text: Express authority

options.label: B
options.text: Implied authority

options.label: C
options.text: Apparent authority

options.label: D
options.text: No authority; the insurer is never bound by unauthorized agent statements

questions.correct_explanation: Apparent authority arises when the insurer's actions or conduct lead a reasonable person to believe the agent has authority to act on the insurer's behalf. Even though the insurer did not expressly or impliedly authorize the specific promise, if the client reasonably relied on the agent's position as the insurer's representative, the insurer may be bound by the agent's statement under apparent authority principles.

wrong_explanations.A: Express authority is authority specifically granted by the insurer to the agent, typically through the agency contract. Since the insurer never authorized this particular promise, express authority does not apply here.
wrong_explanations.B: Implied authority covers acts that are reasonably necessary to carry out expressly granted duties (such as collecting premiums when authorized to deliver policies). Making coverage promises that contradict the policy goes beyond implied authority.
wrong_explanations.D: Insurers can be bound by unauthorized agent statements when apparent authority exists. The law of agency protects third parties who reasonably rely on the agent's perceived authority, even when the insurer did not authorize the specific act.

questions.stem: An applicant for a life insurance policy pays the initial premium at the time of application and receives a conditional receipt. The applicant is later found insurable as applied for. Under the conditional receipt, when does coverage begin?

options.label: A
options.text: On the date the policy is delivered to the applicant

options.label: B
options.text: On the date the insurer issues the policy

options.label: C
options.text: On the date of the application or the date of the medical exam, whichever is later, provided the applicant is found insurable as applied for

options.label: D
options.text: Thirty days after the application is submitted, regardless of underwriting outcome

questions.correct_explanation: A conditional receipt provides that if the applicant pays the initial premium at application and is found insurable as applied for, coverage is effective retroactively to the date of application or the date of the medical exam, whichever is later. This protects the applicant during the underwriting period. If the applicant is not insurable as applied for, the premium is refunded and no coverage was in effect.

wrong_explanations.A: Under a conditional receipt, coverage does not wait for policy delivery. The retroactive effective date is the key advantage of the conditional receipt over simply waiting for delivery.
wrong_explanations.B: The policy issue date is not the controlling date under a conditional receipt. Coverage relates back to the application or medical exam date, not the administrative issuance date.
wrong_explanations.D: The conditional receipt does not provide automatic coverage after 30 days regardless of insurability. The condition is that the applicant must be found insurable as applied for. If not, no coverage existed.

questions.stem: A Texas HMO conducts utilization review to evaluate the medical necessity of proposed treatments. Under Texas law, who must make the final determination that a requested service is not medically necessary?

options.label: A
options.text: A claims examiner with no medical training

options.label: B
options.text: A licensed physician

options.label: C
options.text: The enrollee's employer

options.label: D
options.text: The Texas Department of Insurance commissioner personally

questions.correct_explanation: Texas law requires that adverse determinations regarding medical necessity in utilization review be made by or under the supervision of a licensed physician. A non-physician claims processor may handle administrative screening, but the final decision to deny a service based on medical necessity must involve physician-level review.

wrong_explanations.A: A claims examiner without medical training may perform initial administrative screening, but cannot make the final medical necessity determination. That decision requires physician-level clinical judgment under Texas law.
wrong_explanations.C: The enrollee's employer has no role in utilization review or medical necessity determinations. Those decisions are made within the HMO's clinical review process.
wrong_explanations.D: The TDI commissioner oversees regulatory compliance but does not personally make individual medical necessity determinations for HMO enrollees. That is the function of the HMO's physician reviewers.

questions.stem: An agent tells a prospective client that a competing insurer is facing bankruptcy and will not pay claims, knowing this statement is completely false. This conduct is BEST described as:

options.label: A
options.text: Twisting

options.label: B
options.text: Defamation of an insurer

options.label: C
options.text: Rebating

options.label: D
options.text: Controlled business

questions.correct_explanation: Defamation in insurance occurs when a person makes false, derogatory, or malicious statements about the financial condition of an insurer with the intent to injure that insurer or influence a purchasing decision. Knowingly making a false statement about a competitor's financial stability fits this definition precisely.

wrong_explanations.A: Twisting involves using misrepresentation to induce a policyholder to replace an existing policy. This scenario does not involve replacing an existing policy; it involves disparaging a competing insurer to a prospective new client.
wrong_explanations.C: Rebating involves offering inducements of value not stated in the policy to encourage a purchase. No monetary or material inducement is offered in this scenario.
wrong_explanations.D: Controlled business refers to an agent writing insurance primarily on his or her own life, family members, or close associates. That concept is unrelated to false statements about a competitor.

questions.stem: A small employer offers group health insurance that includes a 90-day probationary period for new employees. A newly hired employee needs medical care during the first 60 days of employment. What is the employee's coverage status?

options.label: A
options.text: The employee is covered immediately upon hire because group plans cannot impose probationary periods

options.label: B
options.text: The employee is not yet eligible for the group plan because the 90-day probationary period has not been satisfied

options.label: C
options.text: The employee is covered but must pay triple the standard premium during the probationary period

options.label: D
options.text: The employee is covered only for emergency services during the probationary period

questions.correct_explanation: A probationary period (also called a waiting period or eligibility waiting period) is the time a new employee must wait before becoming eligible for group health benefits. During this period, the employee is not covered under the group plan. The employee would need to rely on other coverage (individual insurance, COBRA from a prior employer, marketplace coverage, etc.) until the probationary period ends and group coverage begins.

wrong_explanations.A: Group health plans may impose probationary periods for new employees, subject to applicable limits. Under the ACA, the maximum waiting period for group health plans is 90 days. The employer's 90-day period falls within this limit.
wrong_explanations.C: There is no provision for triple-premium coverage during a probationary period. The employee is simply not eligible for group benefits until the probationary period is satisfied.
wrong_explanations.D: The group plan does not provide partial or emergency-only coverage during the probationary period. The employee is not yet a covered member of the plan.

questions.stem: A life insurance policyholder includes a spendthrift clause in the policy. After the insured's death, a creditor of the beneficiary attempts to garnish the death benefit proceeds before they are paid out. Under the spendthrift clause, what is the result?

options.label: A
options.text: The creditor may garnish the full death benefit because life insurance proceeds are always subject to creditor claims

options.label: B
options.text: The death benefit proceeds are protected from the beneficiary's creditors while held by the insurer, but once paid to the beneficiary, the protection generally ceases

options.label: C
options.text: The creditor may garnish 50% of the death benefit and the remaining 50% is protected

options.label: D
options.text: The spendthrift clause permanently shields the entire death benefit from all creditors even after payment to the beneficiary

questions.correct_explanation: A spendthrift clause protects policy proceeds from the beneficiary's creditors while the proceeds are held by the insurer (for example, under a settlement option). Once the proceeds are paid out to the beneficiary and become part of the beneficiary's general assets, the protection under the spendthrift clause generally ceases, and the funds may be subject to creditor claims like any other personal asset.

wrong_explanations.A: Life insurance proceeds held by the insurer under a spendthrift provision are not subject to garnishment by the beneficiary's creditors. The spendthrift clause exists specifically to provide this protection.
wrong_explanations.C: There is no standard 50/50 split provision. The spendthrift clause protects the full amount while it is held by the insurer. The protection is complete while in effect, not partial.
wrong_explanations.D: The spendthrift clause does not provide permanent, lifetime protection against all creditors after payment. Once proceeds are distributed to the beneficiary and commingled with personal assets, the protection under the clause generally ends.

questions.subdomain: Texas Life and Health Guaranty Association
questions.stem: If a life and health insurance company authorized to do business in Texas becomes insolvent, the Texas Life and Health Insurance Guaranty Association provides protection to policyholders. Which of the following BEST describes the purpose of the Guaranty Association?

options.label: A
options.text: To prevent any insurance company in Texas from ever becoming insolvent

options.label: B
options.text: To continue coverage or pay covered claims up to statutory limits for covered policyholders, primarily Texas residents and in limited cases certain nonresidents, of the insolvent insurer

options.label: C
options.text: To refund all premiums ever paid by any policyholder of the insolvent insurer

options.label: D
options.text: To guarantee that every insurer in Texas earns a profit each year

questions.correct_explanation: The Texas Life and Health Insurance Guaranty Association is a statutory safety net that exists to pay benefits and continue coverage, subject to statutory limits, for covered persons when a member insurer becomes impaired or insolvent. The protection is aimed primarily at Texas residents and, in limited circumstances described by statute, certain nonresidents as well. It does not prevent insolvency or guarantee profitability.

wrong_explanations.A: The Guaranty Association does not prevent insolvency. It responds after insolvency occurs by protecting policyholders up to statutory limits. Solvency regulation and financial oversight are separate functions performed by TDI.
wrong_explanations.C: The Association does not refund all premiums ever paid. Its role is to continue coverage or pay covered claims up to the statutory per-person limits, not to make policyholders whole for every premium dollar.
wrong_explanations.D: The Association has no role in guaranteeing insurer profitability. Its sole function is to protect policyholders when a member insurer becomes insolvent.

status_history.notes: Clarified covered-person scope for Texas guaranty association.

questions.stem: An insured is injured in a car accident caused by another driver. The insured's health insurer pays $30,000 in medical claims. The insured then recovers $50,000 from the at-fault driver's liability insurer. Under the subrogation provision in the health policy, what is the health insurer entitled to do?

options.label: A
options.text: Recover the $30,000 it paid from the insured's third-party recovery

options.label: B
options.text: Collect the full $50,000 from the at-fault driver's insurer

options.label: C
options.text: Nothing, because health insurers have no right to recover from third-party settlements

options.label: D
options.text: Cancel the insured's health policy retroactively because a third party was liable

questions.correct_explanation: Subrogation allows the health insurer to recover the amount it paid in claims from the insured's recovery against the responsible third party. Since the health insurer paid $30,000, it is entitled to recover that amount from the insured's $50,000 settlement. This prevents the insured from receiving a double recovery for the same medical expenses.

wrong_explanations.B: The health insurer's subrogation right is limited to the amount it actually paid ($30,000), not the full amount of the insured's recovery. The insured retains the remainder.
wrong_explanations.C: Many health insurance policies include subrogation provisions that explicitly give the insurer the right to recover claim payments from third-party recoveries. This is a standard contractual provision.
wrong_explanations.D: The existence of a liable third party does not give the insurer grounds to retroactively cancel the policy. Subrogation allows recovery of paid claims, not cancellation of coverage.

questions.stem: A policyowner purchases a whole life policy and pays premiums substantially in excess of the 7-pay test limit during the first three years. The policy is classified as a modified endowment contract (MEC). How does MEC status affect distributions from the policy?

options.label: A
options.text: Distributions are always received completely tax-free regardless of how much was contributed

options.label: B
options.text: Distributions are taxed on a last-in, first-out (LIFO) basis, meaning earnings come out first and are taxed as ordinary income, and a 10% penalty may apply if the policyowner is under age 59½

options.label: C
options.text: The policy loses its death benefit and functions only as an investment account

options.label: D
options.text: MEC status has no effect on taxation; all life insurance is taxed identically

questions.correct_explanation: When a life insurance policy is classified as a MEC (by failing the 7-pay test), distributions (including loans and withdrawals) are taxed on a LIFO basis: earnings are deemed to come out first and are taxed as ordinary income. In addition, a 10% tax penalty applies to the taxable portion of distributions if the policyowner is under age 59½. The death benefit remains income-tax-free to the beneficiary, but the living-benefit tax treatment changes significantly.

wrong_explanations.A: Distributions from a MEC are not tax-free. The LIFO treatment means accumulated earnings are taxed first as ordinary income. Only non-MEC policies enjoy first-in, first-out (FIFO) treatment where basis comes out first.
wrong_explanations.C: MEC status does not eliminate the death benefit. The policy remains a life insurance contract with a death benefit. The change is in how loans and withdrawals are taxed during the policyowner's lifetime.
wrong_explanations.D: MEC status has a significant effect on taxation. Non-MEC policies allow loans and withdrawals on a FIFO basis (basis out first, generally tax-free up to basis). MECs use LIFO, making distributions taxable and potentially penalized.

questions.stem: An individual health insurance policy has been in force for four years. The insurer discovers that the insured made a material but non-fraudulent misrepresentation on the application regarding a pre-existing condition. Can the insurer use this misrepresentation to deny a claim?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, because health insurers may deny claims based on application misrepresentation at any time

options.label: B
options.text: Yes, but only if the misrepresentation was fraudulent

options.label: C
options.text: No, because the time limit on certain defenses provision (typically two or three years) has expired, and the insurer can no longer use the misrepresentation to deny a non-fraudulent claim

options.label: D
options.text: No, because misrepresentation is never grounds for denying a health insurance claim

questions.correct_explanation: The time limit on certain defenses provision in health insurance provides that after the policy has been in force for the stated period (commonly two or three years), the insurer may not use a material but non-fraudulent misrepresentation in the application to deny a claim or void the policy. Since this policy has been in force for four years, that time limit has expired.

wrong_explanations.A: Insurers do not have an unlimited window to deny claims based on application misrepresentation. The time limit on certain defenses restricts this right after the policy has been in force for the stated period.
wrong_explanations.B: Fraud may be treated differently from a material but non-fraudulent misrepresentation. This question expressly says the misrepresentation was non-fraudulent, so the time-limit provision bars the insurer from using it after the stated period has passed.
wrong_explanations.D: Misrepresentation is a valid ground for denying claims during the period before the time limit on certain defenses expires. It is not that misrepresentation is never relevant; rather, the insurer's window to use it is limited.

status_history.notes: Resolved ambiguity by specifying the misrepresentation was non-fraudulent.

questions.stem: Under Texas unfair claims settlement practice rules, all of the following are prohibited insurer practices EXCEPT:

options.label: A
options.text: Failing to acknowledge and act promptly on communications about claims

options.label: B
options.text: Requiring an insured to submit a properly documented proof of loss before paying a claim

options.label: C
options.text: Making claim settlements conditioned on the insured accepting less than the amount due under the policy

options.label: D
options.text: Denying claims without conducting a reasonable investigation

questions.correct_explanation: Requiring a properly documented proof of loss is a legitimate and standard claims-handling practice, not a prohibited act. Insurers have the right to request documentation to verify the claim before making payment. The other three options describe practices that violate Texas unfair claims settlement laws.

wrong_explanations.A: Failing to acknowledge and act promptly on claims communications is a prohibited unfair claims practice. Texas law requires insurers to respond to claims in a timely manner.
wrong_explanations.C: Conditioning payment on the insured accepting a reduced amount is a prohibited practice sometimes called lowballing. The insurer must pay what is owed under the policy terms.
wrong_explanations.D: Denying claims without a reasonable investigation is prohibited. The insurer must investigate claims before making a coverage determination.

questions.stem: A whole life insurance policyholder may choose to pay premiums annually, semi-annually, quarterly, or monthly. If the policyowner switches from annual to monthly premium payments, what is the typical effect on total annual premium cost?

options.label: A
options.text: The total annual cost decreases because of the convenience of smaller payments

options.label: B
options.text: The total annual cost increases because the insurer adds a service charge or frequency loading factor to more frequent payment modes

options.label: C
options.text: The total annual cost remains exactly the same regardless of payment frequency

options.label: D
options.text: The insurer refunds a portion of the premium when the policyowner switches to monthly payments

questions.correct_explanation: When a policyowner selects a more frequent payment mode (monthly instead of annual), the insurer typically adds a service charge or frequency loading factor. This means the sum of 12 monthly payments will exceed the single annual premium. The annual mode is the least expensive way to pay because the insurer has use of the full premium from the start of the year.

wrong_explanations.A: More frequent payments do not reduce cost. They increase total annual cost because of the insurer's additional administrative expenses and the lost investment income from not receiving the full annual premium upfront.
wrong_explanations.C: The total annual cost does not remain the same. The frequency loading means the policyowner pays more in aggregate when choosing monthly over annual. Only the per-payment amount is smaller.
wrong_explanations.D: The insurer does not refund a portion of the premium for switching to monthly mode. If anything, the aggregate cost increases due to the loading factor.

questions.stem: A Texas consumer buys a Medicare supplement policy during the consumer's Medicare supplement open enrollment period. The consumer received treatment for diabetes 4 months before the policy's effective date and has no prior creditable coverage. Assuming the insurer imposes the maximum lawful waiting period, when will diabetes-related claims begin to be covered?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediately on the policy's effective date because no waiting period is ever allowed during open enrollment

options.label: B
options.text: After a waiting period of up to 6 months from the policy's effective date

options.label: C
options.text: After a 12-month exclusion period from the policy's effective date

options.label: D
options.text: Never, because pre-existing conditions may be permanently excluded

questions.correct_explanation: In Texas Medicare supplement open enrollment, the company must sell the policy, but it may impose a waiting period of up to six months before covering preexisting conditions unless prior creditable coverage eliminates or reduces that waiting period. Because the consumer had treatment within the previous six months and no prior creditable coverage, the maximum lawful waiting period is up to six months.

wrong_explanations.A: During Texas Medicare supplement open enrollment, guaranteed issuance applies, but a waiting period of up to six months for preexisting conditions may still be imposed in some cases. Open enrollment does not automatically eliminate every waiting period.
wrong_explanations.C: A 12-month preexisting-condition exclusion is not the rule for Texas Medicare supplement open enrollment. The maximum waiting period is up to six months, not 12 months.
wrong_explanations.D: Preexisting conditions may not be permanently excluded in this context. The issue is a temporary waiting period, not a permanent denial of coverage.

status_history.notes: Rewrote outdated individual-health preexisting-condition exclusion item to current Texas Medicare supplement waiting-period rule.

questions.stem: A life insurance policyholder names her husband as primary beneficiary. Both the insured and the husband die in the same automobile accident, and it cannot be determined who died first. The policy contains a common disaster clause requiring the beneficiary to survive the insured by 30 days. What happens to the death benefit?

options.label: A
options.text: The proceeds are paid to the husband's estate because he was the named primary beneficiary

options.label: B
options.text: The proceeds are paid as though the primary beneficiary predeceased the insured, passing to the contingent beneficiary or the insured's estate

options.label: C
options.text: The insurer retains the death benefit permanently

options.label: D
options.text: The proceeds are split equally between the insured's estate and the beneficiary's estate

questions.correct_explanation: The common disaster clause (also called a survivorship clause) requires the beneficiary to survive the insured by a specified period (here, 30 days). If the beneficiary does not survive that period, the proceeds are distributed as if the primary beneficiary had predeceased the insured. If a contingent beneficiary is named and living, that person receives the proceeds. Otherwise, the proceeds go to the insured's estate.

wrong_explanations.A: The common disaster clause prevents this result. Because the husband did not survive the insured by 30 days, he is treated as having predeceased the insured. The proceeds do not go to the husband's estate.
wrong_explanations.C: The insurer does not retain death benefits. The proceeds must be paid according to the policy's beneficiary hierarchy. The common disaster clause simply redirects the payment away from a beneficiary who did not survive the required period.
wrong_explanations.D: There is no standard provision that splits proceeds between estates in a common disaster. The survivorship clause directs the full proceeds to the next beneficiary in the hierarchy.

questions.stem: An insurance company is incorporated in Ohio but holds a certificate of authority to sell insurance in Texas. In Texas, this company is classified as a:

options.label: A
options.text: Domestic insurer

options.label: B
options.text: Foreign insurer

options.label: C
options.text: Alien insurer

options.label: D
options.text: Surplus lines insurer

questions.correct_explanation: An insurer incorporated in another U.S. state (Ohio) but authorized to do business in Texas is classified as a foreign insurer from the Texas perspective. A domestic insurer is one incorporated in Texas. An alien insurer is incorporated in another country. These classifications are based on the insurer's state of domicile relative to the regulating state.

wrong_explanations.A: A domestic insurer is one incorporated in the state where it is being classified. Since this insurer is incorporated in Ohio, it is not a domestic Texas insurer.
wrong_explanations.C: An alien insurer is one incorporated in a country other than the United States. An Ohio-incorporated company is a U.S. company and therefore foreign, not alien, from Texas's perspective.
wrong_explanations.D: A surplus lines insurer is one that writes coverage for risks that admitted (authorized) insurers are unwilling to cover. Classification as foreign, domestic, or alien is based on domicile, not on surplus lines status.

questions.stem: A point-of-service (POS) plan combines features of which two managed care models?

options.label: A
options.text: HMO and PPO

options.label: B
options.text: Indemnity and Workers' Compensation

options.label: C
options.text: Medicare Advantage and Medigap

options.label: D
options.text: COBRA and long-term care insurance

questions.correct_explanation: A point-of-service (POS) plan blends features of an HMO and a PPO. Like an HMO, the POS plan assigns a primary care physician and encourages use of a provider network. Like a PPO, it allows the member to receive care outside the network at a higher out-of-pocket cost. The member chooses at the point of service whether to use in-network (HMO-like) or out-of-network (PPO-like) care.

wrong_explanations.B: Indemnity insurance and Workers' Compensation are fundamentally different products and are not managed care models. A POS plan does not combine these.
wrong_explanations.C: Medicare Advantage and Medigap are Medicare-related products, not the building blocks of a POS plan. While Medicare Advantage may use managed care structures, the POS concept is defined by its HMO/PPO hybrid nature.
wrong_explanations.D: COBRA is a continuation coverage law, and long-term care insurance is a separate product line. Neither is a managed care model that contributes to the POS design.

questions.stem: Insurance contracts are considered contracts of adhesion because:

options.label: A
options.text: Both the insurer and the applicant negotiate every term of the contract equally

options.label: B
options.text: The insurer drafts the contract and the applicant must accept or reject it as written, without negotiating individual terms

options.label: C
options.text: The contract must be physically attached to the application to be valid

options.label: D
options.text: The contract automatically adheres to the laws of every state in the country

questions.correct_explanation: A contract of adhesion is one prepared entirely by one party (the insurer) and offered to the other party (the applicant) on a take-it-or-leave-it basis. The applicant has no opportunity to negotiate individual provisions. Because of this unequal bargaining position, courts interpret any ambiguity in the contract language in favor of the insured (the adhering party).

wrong_explanations.A: Equal negotiation describes an arms-length commercial contract, not a contract of adhesion. Insurance contracts are not individually negotiated between the insurer and each applicant.
wrong_explanations.C: The term 'adhesion' does not refer to physical attachment. It refers to the legal concept that one party adheres (agrees) to terms drafted entirely by the other party.
wrong_explanations.D: Adhesion has nothing to do with multi-state applicability. Insurance contracts are governed by the law of the state where the contract is issued or delivered, not every state.

questions.stem: A Texas HMO enrollee's request for a specific medical procedure is denied as not medically necessary. The enrollee disagrees with the determination. Under Texas law, what is the enrollee's first step in challenging the denial?

options.label: A
options.text: File a lawsuit directly in state district court

options.label: B
options.text: File a complaint with the Federal Trade Commission

options.label: C
options.text: Submit an internal appeal through the HMO's complaint and appeal process

options.label: D
options.text: Cancel the HMO enrollment and switch to an indemnity plan immediately

questions.correct_explanation: Texas law requires HMOs to maintain internal complaint and appeal procedures. When an enrollee disagrees with a coverage denial, the first step is to use the HMO's internal appeal process. If the internal appeal does not resolve the dispute, the enrollee may then pursue external review through an independent review organization (IRO) or file a complaint with the Texas Department of Insurance.

wrong_explanations.A: Filing a lawsuit is generally not the first step. The enrollee should exhaust the HMO's internal appeal process first. Texas law provides for internal appeals followed by independent external review before litigation.
wrong_explanations.B: The Federal Trade Commission does not handle individual HMO medical necessity disputes. Insurance regulation in Texas falls under the Texas Department of Insurance, and the internal HMO appeal is the initial step.
wrong_explanations.D: Canceling enrollment does not resolve the coverage dispute. The enrollee has the right to challenge the denial through the HMO's internal process and, if necessary, through external review.

questions.stem: Under a standard individual health insurance policy, within how many days of a covered loss must the insured submit written proof of loss to the insurer?

options.label: A
options.text: Within 10 days of the loss

options.label: B
options.text: Within 20 days of the loss

options.label: C
options.text: Within 90 days of the loss, but not later than one year (except in the absence of legal capacity)

options.label: D
options.text: There is no time limit for submitting proof of loss

questions.correct_explanation: The standard proof of loss provision in individual health insurance policies requires the insured to submit written proof of loss within 90 days of the date of loss. However, the claim is not barred if proof is filed as late as one year after the deadline, unless the insured lacked legal capacity. This standard is part of the Uniform Individual Accident and Sickness Policy Provisions.

wrong_explanations.A: Ten days is the typical notice of claim requirement (notice that a loss has occurred), not the proof of loss deadline. Proof of loss (the detailed documentation) is due within 90 days.
wrong_explanations.B: Twenty days is the standard period within which the insurer must furnish proof of loss forms after receiving notice of claim. The insured's deadline for submitting completed proof is 90 days from the loss.
wrong_explanations.D: There is a time limit. The standard is 90 days, with a maximum extension of one year except in cases of legal incapacity. Open-ended filing is not permitted under the standard provision.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old applicant requests that her new whole life policy be backdated by four months to obtain a lower premium rate based on her age at that earlier date. The insurer agrees. What limitation typically applies to backdating?

options.label: A
options.text: Backdating is prohibited under all circumstances

options.label: B
options.text: Backdating is permitted, but generally limited to no more than 6 months, and the applicant must pay premiums for the backdated period

options.label: C
options.text: Backdating is permitted for up to 3 years if the applicant can prove she was in good health during the entire period

options.label: D
options.text: Backdating is permitted only for term life policies, not whole life

questions.correct_explanation: Backdating a life insurance policy to obtain a lower premium based on a younger issue age is generally permitted, but most states (including Texas) limit the backdate to no more than 6 months. The applicant must pay premiums for the entire backdated period, meaning the policy is treated as if it were issued on the earlier date. This practice benefits the applicant through a lower age-based premium.

wrong_explanations.A: Backdating is not prohibited. It is a recognized industry practice that benefits applicants by preserving a younger issue age. The practice is regulated, not forbidden.
wrong_explanations.C: A 3-year backdating window would be excessive and is not standard. The typical regulatory limit is 6 months. Longer backdating would create an unreasonable gap between the policy date and the actual issue date.
wrong_explanations.D: Backdating is not limited to term policies. It may be applied to any type of life insurance policy where a younger issue age results in a lower premium, including whole life.

questions.stem: An agent contacts an existing whole life policyowner every two years and convinces her to surrender her current policy's cash value and use it to purchase a new whole life policy from the same insurer. Each time, the policyowner incurs new sales charges and begins a new surrender charge period, while the agent earns a new first-year commission. This repeated pattern is BEST described as:

options.label: A
options.text: Conservation

options.label: B
options.text: Churning

options.label: C
options.text: Rebating

options.label: D
options.text: A legitimate portfolio rebalancing strategy

questions.correct_explanation: Churning occurs when an agent repeatedly replaces or exchanges a client's insurance policies primarily to generate new commissions, without genuine benefit to the policyholder. Each replacement resets surrender charges and costs the policyowner cash value. The pattern of repeated surrenders and repurchases with the same insurer, driven by the agent's commission interest rather than the client's benefit, is the hallmark of churning.

wrong_explanations.A: Conservation is the opposite of this conduct. Conservation means encouraging a policyholder to keep an existing policy rather than replacing it. This agent is actively inducing replacements.
wrong_explanations.C: Rebating involves offering inducements of value not specified in the policy to encourage purchase. The misconduct here is repeated unnecessary replacement for commissions, not offering prohibited inducements.
wrong_explanations.D: Repeatedly surrendering and repurchasing whole life policies from the same insurer with new sales charges and surrender periods does not constitute legitimate financial planning. It benefits the agent at the policyowner's expense.

questions.stem: A Texas HMO enrollee has exhausted the HMO's internal appeal process and the denial of coverage for a recommended medical procedure has been upheld. Under Texas law, what is the enrollee's next step?

options.label: A
options.text: The enrollee has no further recourse and must accept the denial

options.label: B
options.text: The enrollee may request an independent review by an independent review organization (IRO) assigned by TDI, which will conduct a de novo evaluation of the medical necessity determination

options.label: C
options.text: The enrollee must restart the internal appeal from the beginning with the same HMO

options.label: D
options.text: The enrollee may only appeal if the denied procedure costs more than $10,000

questions.correct_explanation: Texas law provides that after a covered person exhausts the HMO's internal appeal, the person may request an independent review through an independent review organization (IRO). TDI assigns the IRO, which conducts a fresh (de novo) evaluation of whether the denied service is medically necessary. The IRO's determination is binding on the HMO. This external review process is a key consumer protection in Texas managed care regulation.

wrong_explanations.A: Texas law provides external review as a further recourse after the internal appeal is exhausted. The enrollee is not left without options after the HMO's internal process.
wrong_explanations.C: Restarting the internal appeal with the same HMO would not constitute meaningful review. Texas law provides for external, independent review by an organization that has no affiliation with the HMO.
wrong_explanations.D: There is no $10,000 threshold for requesting independent review. The right to external review applies to covered persons who have exhausted internal appeals, regardless of the dollar amount of the denied procedure.
